original_abstract:
Safely capturing and sequestering radiological iodine ($^{129}$I) is a critical requirement for the advancement of safe nuclear energy and waste management. While silver-containing mordenite (MOR) has long served as the benchmark material for iodine capture, its performance in the complex gas streams typical of nuclear fuel reprocessing remains a subject of intense study. Specifically, the molecular-level mechanisms governing the sorption and decomposition of organic iodine compounds, such as methyl iodide (CH$_3$I), are not yet fully understood.

In this study, we investigate the structural evolution and distribution of methyl iodide within silver-exchanged MOR using a multifaceted analytical approach. By combining infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis-mass spectrometry (TGA-MS), micro-X-ray fluorescence ($\mu$-XRF), powder X-ray diffraction, and pair distribution function (PDF) analysis, we provide a detailed picture of the capture process. 

Our results demonstrate that iodine is chemically and physically sequestered within the mordenite pores as AgI nanoparticles, whose dimensions are constrained by the zeolite framework. Simultaneously, the organic component of the molecule undergoes surface-catalyzed decomposition. We identify the formation of Surface Methoxy Species (SMS) at the zeolite's acid sites, which act as intermediates in the production of downstream organic byproducts, specifically dimethyl ether and methanol. These findings highlight the dual role of Ag-MOR as both a chemical sorbent and a catalyst, providing essential insights for the design of more effective materials for nuclear atmospheric protection.
humanized_abstract:
Radiological iodine ($^{129}$I) capture and storage must be carried out in a safe manner for the future of nuclear energy and waste management. Although silver containing mordenite (MOR) has been the reference material for iodine sorption, the viability of this material in the harsh conditions of the off-gases of nuclear fuel reprocessing plants is under investigation. In particular, little is known about the molecular events occurring during the sorption and subsequent degradation of organic iodides such as methyl iodide (CH$_3$I).

Methyl iodide incorporation in silver exchanged MOR zeolites: Structural evolution and methyl iodide distribution investigated by combined IR, TGA-MS, μ-XRF, XRD and PDF techniques In this study the structural evolution and the methyl iodide distribution within the channels of silver-exchanged MOR zeolites and the adsorption mechanism of methyl iodide (MeI) have been investigated by means of a suite of complementary techniques including infrared spectroscopy, TGA-MS, μ-XRF, XRD and PDF analysis.

Iodine incorporated in the Silver-mordenite (Ag-MOR) is localised in the mordenite pores as AgI nanoparticles whose size is controlled by the structure of the zeolite. The organic part of the complex is degraded by surface catalytic reactions producing Surface Methoxy Species (SMS) located at the acid sites of the zeolite which act as precursors for further secondary organic products such as dimethyl ether and methanol. Overall this study sheds light on the fate of iodine species loaded into Ag-MOR, indicating that Ag-MOR behaves as a chemical sorbent and catalyst for safeguarding nuclear atmospheric releases.

document.input:
Safely capturing and sequestering radiological iodine ($^{129}$I) is a critical requirement for the advancement of safe nuclear energy and waste management. While silver-containing mordenite (MOR) has long served as the benchmark material for iodine capture, its performance in the complex gas streams typical of nuclear fuel reprocessing remains a subject of intense study.
